quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "FTC Part 1"?

options.text: A point where concavity changes; f''(x) = 0 or undefined AND the sign of f''(x) changes
To find absolute extrema on [a,b]: 1) find critical points, 2) evaluate f at critical points and endpoints, 3) compare values
d/dx[∫ₐˣ f(t)dt] = f(x); differentiating the integral gives back the original function
0/0, ∞/∞, 0·∞, ∞-∞, 0⁰, 1^∞, ∞⁰; L'Hôpital's directly handles the first two

quiz_questions.explanation: "FTC Part 1" is defined as: d/dx[∫ₐˣ f(t)dt] = f(x); differentiating the integral gives back the original function. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "FTC Part 2"?

options.text: ∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx = F(b) - F(a); the definite integral equals the net change in any antiderivative
A function can have more than one point satisfying MVT; each is where the tangent is parallel to the secant
f'(x) > 0: f is increasing (quantity growing); f'(x) < 0: f is decreasing (quantity declining)
If f' changes from + to - at c: local maximum; from - to +: local minimum; no sign change: neither

quiz_questions.explanation: "FTC Part 2" is defined as: ∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx = F(b) - F(a); the definite integral equals the net change in any antiderivative. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Accumulation function"?

options.text: F(x) = ∫ₐˣ f(t)dt; represents the accumulated area from a to x; its derivative is f(x) by FTC Part 1
State the integral being evaluated, show the antiderivative, substitute limits, and simplify; credit for setup even if computation errors occur
∫f'(x)/f(x)dx = ln|f(x)| + C (u = f(x)); ∫f'(x)·[f(x)]ⁿdx = [f(x)]ⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C
The set of input values that make physical sense in the context; endpoints may produce absolute extrema

quiz_questions.explanation: "Accumulation function" is defined as: F(x) = ∫ₐˣ f(t)dt; represents the accumulated area from a to x; its derivative is f(x) by FTC Part 1. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "FTC with chain rule"?

options.text: A function can have more than one point satisfying MVT; each is where the tangent is parallel to the secant
For triply composed functions: d/dx[f(g(h(x)))] = f'(g(h(x))) · g'(h(x)) · h'(x)
f' gives the topology (hills and valleys); f'' gives the shape (cupping up or down); together they determine the complete curve shape
d/dx[∫ₐ^(g(x)) f(t)dt] = f(g(x))·g'(x); apply chain rule when the upper limit is a function of x

quiz_questions.explanation: "FTC with chain rule" is defined as: d/dx[∫ₐ^(g(x)) f(t)dt] = f(g(x))·g'(x); apply chain rule when the upper limit is a function of x. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Net change interpretation"?

options.text: = e^(f(x)) · f'(x); the most common chain rule application with exponentials
If f'(x) = 0 for all x in an interval, MVT proves f must be a constant function on that interval
∫ₐᵇ f'(x)dx = f(b) - f(a); the integral of a rate of change gives the net change over [a,b]
Choose h measuring depth down from the surface (h = 0 at surface); or set up y-axis and convert

quiz_questions.explanation: "Net change interpretation" is defined as: ∫ₐᵇ f'(x)dx = f(b) - f(a); the integral of a rate of change gives the net change over [a,b]. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Fundamental Theorem of Calculus is accurate?

options.text: The method: (1) identify the quantity to optimize, (2) express it as a function of one variable using constraints, (3) find critical points where f'(x) = 0, (4) verify maxima/minima using the first or second derivative test, (5) check endpoints...
Second Derivative Test provides a quick classification of critical points using concavity.
Fundamental Theorem of Calculus (FTC) connects differentiation and integration as inverse operations.
Mean Value Theorem: Applications and Consequences explores the theoretical power of MVT.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Fundamental Theorem of Calculus as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Fundamental Theorem of Calculus?

options.text: Part 1: if f is continuous, then d/dx[∫ₐˣ f(t)dt] = f(x)—the derivative of an accumulation function equals the integrand.
The Mean Value Theorem for Integrals guarantees there exists a c in [a,b] where f(c) = f_avg—the function actually achieves its average value at some point.
This yields the derivatives of inverse trigonometric functions: d/dx[arcsin x] = 1/√(1-x²), d/dx[arccos x] = -1/√(1-x²), d/dx[arctan x] = 1/(1+x²).
If f'(x) > 0 on an interval, then f is increasing on that interval; if f'(x) < 0, f is decreasing.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Fundamental Theorem of Calculus as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Fundamental Theorem of Calculus?

options.text: Part 2: ∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx = F(b) - F(a), where F is any antiderivative of f—exact area computation using antiderivatives.
The horizontal line test determines if a function is one-to-one.
Understanding growth rates is essential for comparing algorithms, modeling populations, and analyzing asymptotic behavior.
It is the theoretical foundation for many calculus results.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Fundamental Theorem of Calculus as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Fundamental Theorem of Calculus?

options.text: Arc Length and Surface Area uses integration to measure curve length and rotational surface area.
The indefinite integral ∫f(x)dx = F(x) + C, where C is the constant of integration (since derivatives of constants are zero).
FTC Part 1 with chain rule: d/dx[∫ₐ^(g(x)) f(t)dt] = f(g(x))·g'(x).
It is the theoretical foundation for many calculus results.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Fundamental Theorem of Calculus as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Fundamental Theorem of Calculus?

options.text: Drug dosing models use DEs to track drug concentration in the bloodstream.
Trigonometric substitutions handle expressions with √(a²-x²), √(a²+x²), or √(x²-a²).
Combined with the chain rule: d/dx[e^(g(x))] = e^(g(x)) · g'(x) and d/dx[ln(g(x))] = g'(x)/g(x).
Fundamental Theorem of Calculus (FTC) connects differentiation and integration as inverse operations.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Fundamental Theorem of Calculus as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Fundamental Theorem of Calculus?

options.text: Computing total displacement from velocity: ∫₀ᵀ v(t)dt = s(T) - s(0), where the integral of velocity rate gives the net position change
Traffic analysis: given a graph of vehicle entry rate, computing total vehicles and identifying peak congestion periods
Population dynamics: given a birth rate minus death rate function, calculate total population change over a decade
Acceleration as the second derivative of position: a car speeding up has positive s''(t), slowing down has negative s''(t), and constant speed has s''(t) = 0

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Fundamental Theorem of Calculus?

options.text: Net income calculation: integrating the rate of earnings minus expenses over a fiscal year gives the total profit for the year
Numerical integration in computer science: trapezoidal and Simpson's rules are programmed into calculators and software for approximating integrals when antiderivatives don't exist in closed form
Disease spread: the SIR model's infection curve resembles logistic growth, with the inflection point marking peak new infections
Profit maximization: after finding the critical point of P(x), checking P''(x) < 0 confirms it's a maximum profit level, not a minimum

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Fundamental Theorem of Calculus applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Radiocarbon dating: ¹⁴C decays with t₁/₂ ≈ 5,730 years; measuring remaining ¹⁴C in organic material determines its age up to ~50,000 years
FTC in engineering: the work done by a variable force F(x) over a distance is W = ∫ₐᵇ F(x)dx = potential energy change
Trajectory optimization: confirming that a critical point of a fuel consumption function represents minimum fuel use (f'' > 0) rather than maximum
Orbital mechanics: computing orbital periods and trajectories involves integrals with expressions like √(a²-x²), solved with trigonometric substitution

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Fundamental Theorem of Calculus?

options.text: Error analysis in numerical methods: understanding limits helps predict how rounding errors accumulate as step sizes approach zero in computer calculations
Computing total displacement from velocity: ∫₀ᵀ v(t)dt = s(T) - s(0), where the integral of velocity rate gives the net position change
Electrical engineering: integrating sinusoidal voltage V(t) = V₀sin(ωt) across a capacitor to find charge: Q = ∫V dt = -(V₀/ω)cos(ωt) + C
Rocket propulsion: as fuel is burned, the rocket becomes lighter, changing the force equation—integration handles the continuously varying mass

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Fundamental Theorem of Calculus in practice?

options.text: Net income calculation: integrating the rate of earnings minus expenses over a fiscal year gives the total profit for the year
Finding the slope of a circle x² + y² = 25 at any point: dy/dx = -x/y, giving the tangent line direction for navigation and engineering applications
Orbital mechanics: computing orbital periods and trajectories involves integrals with expressions like √(a²-x²), solved with trigonometric substitution
Surveillance camera tracking: as a car drives past a fixed camera, the rate of angle change dθ/dt determines how fast the camera must pan to keep the car in frame

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.
